data.title: A 13% Yield Coiled Spring Ready To Pop: BrightSpire Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Farmers have a complex task of deciding what to grow and identifying the niche they want to enter. If you look up the 15 most successful and profitable crops for small farms, the vast majority of them have to be planted every single year and grow to full size within 30 to 90 days. This means that much of the year, your farm will either need to be replanted and growing again if you live somewhere in the south where that is possible, or your farm may be idle and need an alternative source of income.

You can get on average between $1.50 and $5 per pound of lettuce. Perhaps, though, if you want to be a farmer who wants to think a little longer term, you could plant an orchard of cherry trees, which gather $1.50-$5 per pound, and the average tree can produce 100-150 pounds of cherries per year. This would be something where you plant it once and continue to harvest the produce from it over decades. You first, though, have to have the capital and the sustainability to afford waiting for your trees to reach fruiting age and maximum production.

When it comes to building a portfolio in the stock market, I'm a great believer in investing in immediate income investments. This means that my investments are paying me a high yield now. This enables me to invest in opportunities that are higher yielding but may not bear full fruit and benefit for a couple of years out. Unlike many traders who buy something and plan to sell within 6 to 12 months or even less, I buy and hold for years. This means that I'm willing to buy something that is in the germination stage of development and allow it to grow in my Portfolio, carefully watched over.

Today, I want to look at an opportunity that carries a great yield now, but the real benefits of holding it won't come to fruition for potentially a couple of years from now.

Let's dive in!

Pushing Down On A Coiled Spring

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (NYSE:BRSP), yielding 13%, is a commercial mortgage REIT. In theory, commercial mortgage REITs thrive in high-interest-rate environments because they are invested in variable debt. However, a combination of rapidly rising rates and weakening real estate fundamentals led to numerous defaults. Many commercial mortgage REITs were unable to leverage up and take advantage of higher rates, needing to be more cautious to manage their non-accruals.

In the earnings call, CEO Mike Mazzei was straightforward with what will be needed to get the dividend to $0.20/quarter:

"Let me make this a lot easier. What we need to do to get back to circa $0.20 a share is we need to get the portfolio back up to $3.5 billion. And right now, it stands at $2.4 billion. So, we need to keep abreast of payments, and we have to have a net gain on the portfolio between now and, let's say, a year from now of about $1 billion net gain to about $3.5 billion, which would bring us up to about low 3 times leverage, 3.3 times leverage on the portfolio if we get there."

There are two aspects to the size of the portfolio. First, there is the amount that BRSP is capable of originating. Originating a mortgage requires finding someone who wants a mortgage, underwriting the building, negotiating the loan, and then the deal actually closing and being funded. Sometimes, a lender might work to originate a loan for an acquisition, but then the real estate sale never happens. The lender typically doesn't have a lot of say on when a closing happens and has no power to accelerate it. So, BRSP's ability to originate mortgages is determined by the opportunities presented, those opportunities being acceptable to BRSP and the underlying real estate transactions closing. BRSP can control its own activity in terms of putting itself out there as a lender open to business, but the level of others' activity is out of its control.

The second factor is the loans that are being paid off or are in default. Source

In Q1, BRSP funded $116 million in loans, but there were $133 million in repayments, and $33 million was a loan that was foreclosed on, and the value was moved to REO (Real Estate Owned). As a result, the overall size of the loan portfolio shrank.

In Q2, we expect that BRSP's portfolio will shrink more as it resolved its largest non-performing loan. The San Jose hotel was foreclosed on and transferred into BRSP's possession in June. That hotel was carried at a value of $135 million, and will be moved into REO with a new value of $80 million – the price that BRSP bid at the foreclosure auction.

BRSP had two properties on non-accrual, with the San Jose loan being the largest one:

As a result of that transaction, BRSP will see a decline in book value because it will record a $55 million "loss", and its loan portfolio will shrink. However, in terms of cash flow, BRSP will start collecting the net income produced by the hotel. It was collecting nothing, now it will be collecting something. At some point in the future, BRSP will sell the Signia Hotel, and it will realize a gain or loss upon the $80 million value. At the Q2 earnings call, we expect management to discuss how it plans to maximize the value of the property to sell it for a higher price.

So, for Q2, we don't expect BRSP to make substantial progress in growing its portfolio on paper. We expect that book value will decline due to the write-off caused by the hotel being moved from a loan to being owned real estate. We expect that cash flow will be relatively unchanged in Q2.

On the positive side, BRSP's non-accrual rate is going to be dramatically lower, and its watchlist will account for a much smaller portion of its portfolio. Knowing what is happening with the Signia Hotel and knowing that BRSP will start seeing cash flow from that property in Q3 is a positive. The amount of uncertainty surrounding BRSP is now much lower, and that is typically a good thing for the market.

The bottom line is that we don't think BRSP is going to build up an extra $1 billion this year. We expect that the dividend will remain at $0.16/quarter. We further expect that we will have to wait until Q3 to start seeing some positive growth in the loan portfolio. BRSP has spent most of the past year in the $5-$6 range, and we expect that to continue for the next few months. I'm very happy to be buying at these prices, getting a healthy yield today with an eye on returning to that $0.20/quarter dividend probably in late 2026 or early 2027

Conclusion

At current prices, BRSP offers an attractive 13% yield for those who are buying shares at these prices. The dividend remains a question of what will occur in the future, but the company's CEO has been extremely open. The dividend isn't increasing until the portfolio size is large enough to support it. This makes sense since BRSP is an REIT and is required to distribute most of its taxable earnings as dividends to its shareholders. There is no need to pay an oversized dividend that they can't afford, only to dwindle the ability to pay it in the future. Likewise, the company needs to expand its portfolio to sustain dividend growth. Currently, BRSP is in the trenches, along with many other companies exposed to commercial real estate, as they seek the best path forward for their holdings. We highlighted previously how another wonderful commercial real estate REIT is switching to an aggressive offensive footing after being careful navigating the current environment, and BRSP is starting to position itself that way as well.

Your retirement is going to hopefully be about 30 years, if not longer. This means that you shouldn't invest only with a 1-2 year mindset, but rather with a mindset that considers decades of future needs and benefits to enjoy. I collect income today to reinvest it and to enjoy it today with an eye on the future. My portfolio will continue to provide me with a wonderful income over those 30 years, and I can also continue to grow that income over time. This is why my portfolio of income investments found within High Dividend Opportunities is designed to provide income growth as well as strong portfolio guidance rules that ensure a high degree of diversification and a reduction of risk where possible. Your portfolio should be designed to provide for you today and tomorrow. Unfortunately, too many investors focus on just tomorrow and forget today, or go all in on today and forget tomorrow. The balance for you is going to be what can make you comfortable in both situations, and building a portfolio to meet that.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Solesence Is Growing A Lot, But The Stock Price Is Several Years In The Future
data.description:
Solesence (SLSN) is a cosmetics CDMO that manufactures for skin-care third-party brands. The company's consumer-product segment (called Solesence) has been incredibly successful, multiplying revenues by 20x in the past four years.

On the margin side, the company has not been super able to leverage production margins, but has been able to control SG&A very positively, translating that revenue growth into some profitability growth.

The company also ticks other quality boxes, like low leverage and a strong controlling shareholder.

On the other hand, the company's valuation is certainly not low. The name trades at 5x sales or more, and even assuming significant growth going forward, it would be years before it could print even a moderate earnings yield. I think the market is a little ahead of developments, and therefore initiate the name with a Hold despite its very interesting characteristics.

From Ingredients To Finished Products

For many years, Solesence traded under the name Nanophase Technologies, with the ticker NANX.

The company's business had been the manufacturing of certain specialty chemicals that are used in cosmetics. For years, the company's main client had been BASF, under which the company manufactured zinc and titanium oxide products (see Zinc Oxide Supply Agreement on the latest 10-K). In 2019, BASF represented 65% of the company's revenues.

Then, in 2016, the company launched a new segment called Solesence, which also offered CDMO services, but instead of offering ingredients, manufactured finished products.

By 2020, Solesence (the segment) had grown from zero to about $2 million in revenues, and from then on, its revenues exploded. Today, Solesence (segment) represents 80% of the company's revenues, and the company adopted the name of the segment.

End Demand Is Hard To Evaluate

The company has mentioned some client names, like Colorescience, Tatcha, and Credo (all from the 1Q25 earnings call).

We also know from the company's 10-K for FY24 that concentration is relatively high. One customer represented 32% of sales, and another one 7% of sales (meaning between these two, about half of the finished product segment sales).

When I search for the brand relevance of these customers on Google Trends, they do not show such explosive growth, not even close. It might be that Solesence has gained a consistently larger share of these customers, manufacturing, and that therefore growth comes not as much from end-demand as from share of pocket.

Margin Leverage With Challenges

In terms of margins, Solesence has shown positive dynamics.

On the one hand, gross margins have been pretty flat, despite a tripling of revenues in the last four years and a shift to a more downstream segment. I do not think this is problematic, but rather the contrary.

Ramping production is hard, and companies may find challenges in production. For example, in FY22, the company's gross and operating margins fell significantly (moving to operational losses, see below) because of operational challenges with ramp-up. In 1Q25, something similar happened, as commented on their call. Judging by the comments from analysts and shareholders on the 1Q25 call, the operational challenges have been a constant problem. I do not think this is strange for a company growing the revenues of a segment by 20x in a few years.

Once Solesence's revenues stabilize, I believe their gross margins can grow via efficiencies.

Even more positive, Solesence's OpEx has remained very well controlled. Indeed, OpEx has grown, but at a compounded rate that is 1/3 of revenues. Further, in the past two years, OpEx has been fairly flat while revenues doubled again.

Controlled OpEx is the basis of profitability in a volume business like CDMO, because it allows the business to post operational leverage. Unsurprisingly, Solesence has moved from consistent negative profitability to breakeven and then to profits with margins of almost 10%.

Committed Management And Ownership

The company's CEO joined the company in 2009. As the company was small back then, he also filled the roles of CFO, to this day. Salary and compensation are relatively moderate. For example, in 2023 and 2024, each CEO and COO received about $400 thousand each, with very little in the form of bonuses. In many companies, growing revenues 100% twice, managers tend to receive large stock prizes (see the latest proxy).

This control over expenses and over management might come from the company's controlling shareholder. One of the company's shareholders owned 72% of the company in 2024, and his wife is the Chairman of the Board. The Board has three members only (one is the CEO), and made less than $100 thousand in total compensation in 2024.

I generally like companies with managements and Boards that are paid correctly but not excessively, and with controlling shareholders that have a lot of skin in the game.

External Financing Might Be Needed

As of 1Q25, Solesence cash balances were $1.8 million, added total current assets of about $34 million, against current liabilities of $33 million, and no long-term debt.

The company has access to two lines of credit, but the exact amount is not clear. From the 10-K liquidity section, I calculate about $13 million, which means about $6 million is already in use.

It should be noted that the companies providing the credit lines to Solesence are related to the company's largest shareholder (see Item 10 of the 10-K).

Further, last year alone, the company required an equity injection, provided by one of the line of credit providers. Strandler (one of the lenders) bought 15,000 preferred shares at $400 each ($6 million in financing). The shares were converted into 15 million common shares in late 2024. At the time of the purchase of the preferred, the company's stock price was about $0.5 to $0.6 per common, meaning each preferred had a conversion value of $500 to $600, and was therefore issued at a discount. This is normal considering that the deal was large.

The company has been operationally profitable for four out of the past five quarters, but it has significant capital requirements. As will be seen below, the company needs to grow equity at about the same rate as it grows revenues. For example, if it grows at 25% per year (currently growing at 50% per year), its equity needs to grow by that same rate (in this case, about $4 million).

The company currently cannot finance that from operations alone (TTM net income is less than $3.5 million, and the company is growing at 50%). The company can use the working capital facilities, but without additional expansions or additions to those facilities, it may need to issue equity again, or slow down its growth.

This dynamic should be monitored over time.

The valuation is hard to justify, even with tremendous optimism

If I have spoken so positively of Solesence, then why is my rating a Hold? Because of valuation, which is the balance of the company's quality in any stock.

Just as it happens with many high-growth names, Solesence's stock price reflects not only its current quality characteristics, but also a lot of what will happen in the future. The problem with such an approach is that performing well becomes a necessity, and not an opportunity. If the company does not meet a very high bar, then the stock price might crater, even if the company's operations are improving.

To understand how big this optimism is in Solesence's price, a simple profitability model will suffice. The company has a market cap of $330 million. Let us calculate how much growth would be needed to obtain an FCF yield of 5% on the name, which, added to a 10% growth yield, would represent a relatively attractive 15% total return yield. This is, assuming fixed earnings multiples, and with a subjective determination of an attractive yield, based on what I believe a high growth but also still small and nascent name should return. The attractive yield number is much less important than the calculations showing how much optimism is needed to reach that yield.

Let us consider that the company can grow gross margins to 30%, from the current ~25%, and above what has been its historical average. Further, let us consider that from the current 20%, OpEx stabilizes around 15%. OpEx leverage cannot be maintained forever in almost no company, because overhead is always required. Still, this would imply a company with an operating margin of 15%, which can be considered a high margin.

In terms of capital, despite tremendous growth in revenues, Solesence's asset utilization has been flattish, and it has not been able to finance that growth via working capital (otherwise, assets to equity would have grown), meaning the company needs to invest at least the same amount as it grows. For example, if the company grows 10%, its equity will need to grow 10% to sustain working capital and PP&E needs.

Solesence has about $12 million in debt and pays around 7% on it, for a total cost of about $700 thousand per year. Assuming the company maintains that level of financing going forward (credit facilities approximately in size with equity or 1/3 of revenues at constant asset utilization, paying 7%), it should devote about 2% of revenues to interest payments.

After interest and taxes of 25%, the company's net margin would be about 10%. With equity representing approximately 1/3 of revenues, the growth of 10% requires approximately 3% of revenues every year. That means the after-tax, after capital investment margin (an FCF margin) would be about 7% of revenues.

Of course, these are speculative figures, but I believe they make sense as an estimation of the company's potential economics. Above all, I believe they are rather optimistic, more than conservative, as they assume operating margins of about 15%.

Now, to generate a 5% yield on its current market cap of $330 million, Solesence would need to generate an FCF of $16.5 million. Based on my calculations above, that figure can be achieved with revenues of $235 million (based on an FCF margin after growth of 8%).

That means the company's stock price already incorporates more than 4x the revenues the company generates today. Even growing at 50% per year, the company's current YoY growth rate, it would take more than three years to grow that much.

That is, Solesence is in some way already pricing more than 300% revenue growth, double its operating margins, and 10% growth going forward. If some of those do not become a reality, then the stock price might suffer. Further, if these are met, only then is the valuation attractive.

For that reason, in my opinion, Solesence is not attractive at these prices. The company is performing very well, but the stock price has gone too far into the future.

data.title: Golub Capital: This Is Why We Are Likely To Talk About Dividend Cut Soon
data.description:
As we entered 2025, I issued an article comparing two rather popular BDCs, which operate mostly in the traditional first-lien space: Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and Golub Capital BDC (GBDC).

The idea of the article was to compare them both side by side in order to determine, which has superior risk-adjusted return potential going forward.

Even though the GBDC had much more favorable valuation, I still opted to go long BXSL. There were multiple drivers behind that, but the key one was the lack of sufficient margin of safety on GBDC's base dividend coverage front.

Here in the chart below, we can see that since the publication of my piece, GBDC has slightly underperformed both BXSL and the broader BDC market (BIZD).

If we strip out the collected dividends, GBDC is actually down by almost 2%, which, theoretically, could suggest that the entry point has become a bit more attractive for investors. Plus, the difference between BXSL's P/NAV and that of GBDC has widened to roughly 15%. Currently, GBDC's P/NAV is 1.03x, which implies that we are not paying too much of a premium (although, compared to sector median, we are paying 8% more).

Another noteworthy aspect is the combination of the current dividend yield of 11.4% (a very attractive level) and the fact that GBDC has managed to accommodate rather stable dividend streams all the way starting in 2010, when it tapped the IPO market. Here is a visual representation of that.

So, at least from the surface, GBDC's investment case looks quite enticing - i.e., an acceptable P/NAV and ~11.4% yield with, theoretically (based on history), the necessary income durability in place.

Let me, however, highlight several points why I am actually not that bullish on this case.

Thesis Review

First of all, we have to understand that by buying GBDC, we are agreeing to pay exactly the price that is associated with the underlying NAV. In fact, we are paying a slight premium, especially compared to the sector median.

Of course, there are such players as Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) that carry more than 35% premiums to their reported NAVs, which suggests that paying extra is not necessarily a reason to not invest. Namely, provided that the right fundamentals are there, it makes sense to pay more.

However, most of these BDCs, which trade above NAV, are associated with internal management schemes, high equity investment exposures, very conservative debt loads, or distinct advantages in the origination front - think of Ares Capital (ARCC).

GBDC has none of these characteristics.

Granted, this is why we are not paying a notable premium to NAV, but then again - I would like to actually see some form of a discount for GBDC.

Here is why.

Since the publication of my prior article on GBDC, the NAV per share value has been consistently declining. In all of the four previously reported financial quarters, GBDC has registered a NAV decay.

The reason is simple - it is a combination of non-performing loan write-downs and declining spreads. The former is a BDC-specific issue and the latter is something that is of a systematic nature, where we should not blame the management.

So, if the NAV per share continues to slide down quarter by quarter, then by buying GBDC now at a slight premium, we are actually assuming a larger (forward-looking) premium, which again questions the overall case here.

Now, it might not be an issue provided that GBDC can halt the NAV-erosion process and, more importantly, keep the current dividends intact in a sustainable and long-term fashion.

And this is where I become skeptical.

First, currently, GBDC has its dividend just barely covered by the NII per share generation. The base dividend coverage ratio stands at 100%, which a) does not leave any margin of safety in place and b) is more aggressive than the sector average of 103% (and remember, the sector average is comprised of many underperforming/speculative cases).

Second, I do not see how GBDC could get out of this situation unless there is a notable change in the systematic drivers, which is clearly out of GBDC's control (rising spreads, higher base rates, sharply recovering M&A markets). The biggest headwind is the forthcoming pressures associated with declining spreads. As of the most recent financial report, GBDC has a weighted average portfolio investment yield of 10.8%, while the new loans that are coming in and gradually replacing the old ones are yielding below 10% (9.4% and 9.7% for Q1 and Q2, 2025, respectively).

Third, there are actually two ways to work yourself out of this kind of a situation. The first one is through a considerable portfolio growth, where the "volume" aspect could offset the "price" aspect of the business. Here I have to mention my favorite BDC - Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) - which has one of the least leveraged balance sheets in the sector, allowing it to employ a "volume-focused" strategy. The second is monetizing high-quality equity stakes in portfolio investment companies. This is where the previously mentioned BDCs - MAIN and CSWC - thrive. However, in GBDC's case we see a debt-saturated balance sheet that has a higher debt load than for the sector average peer. Plus, GBDC has not strong enough equity investment exposure to rely on this strategy (plus, a large part of the equity investments has stemmed from first-lien non-accrual restructuring).

Finally, GBDC has two considerable unsecured notes with clearly below market-level yields maturing in 2026 and 2027 - 2.5% and 2.0%, respectively. Together they both comprise ~19% of the total leverage, which is something that will definitely move the needle one through refinancings the yields would come closer to the 5.5% - 6.5%, which is where the prevailing investment grade BDC cost of debt levels are.

The Bottom Line

If I put all of these pieces together, I clearly struggle to see a justified investment case. It seems that GBDC's earnings are set to go down at least in the short to medium-term. And I have not even factored in the consequences from potentially decreasing base rates and incremental non-accruals, which would not be that surprising given the overall uncertainty in the system.

In my humble opinion, we will see a further NII per share reduction in the upcoming Q3, 2025 earnings report, driven by a continued spread compression and negative net investment volumes in Q2 (that lowered the portfolio base). This would then send the base dividend coverage level below 100%, which should give a substantiated ground for the market to initiate some form of repricing to the downside.

Having said that, given GBDC's dividend distribution track record, I do not see a dividend cut risk now. The BDC has a meaningful amount of undistributed spillover income that it could use to avoid the cut. However, this is inherently a temporary (debt-driven) solution. In my view, we will be talking about a potential dividend cut if and/or when the FED cuts interest rates by at least 50 basis points - i.e., it would drive down the NII into an even more unsustainable territory.

So far, GBDC is not a buy for me.

data.title: Crypto ETFs: Regulation, Returns And Rise Of Innovation
data.description:
The past week was a significant moment for the crypto landscape, both in terms of legislation and market momentum. While not all the regulatory developments directly impact crypto ETFs, the effect of increased regulatory clarity and institutional adoption benefits the space broadly. Here’s a closer look at the evolving regulatory backdrop, fund flows, and new product developments across the crypto ETF ecosystem.

More regulatory support…

Last week was dubbed “Crypto Week.” Three major bills made headlines: the GENIUS Act, the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, and the Clarity Act. Each of these represents a step toward building a more defined framework for digital assets. That is something that has been a persistent hurdle for the industry. Several of these bills are loosely related to crypto ETFs. Regardless, regulatory progress is contributing to overall optimistic sentiment in this space.

  • The GENIUS Act (Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins) addresses the issuance and oversight of stablecoins. This act has officially been signed into law. Ethereum (ETH-USD) plays a major role in stablecoin operations and has reacted positively to this news.
  • The Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act prevents the Federal Reserve from issuing a central bank digital currency (CBDC) directly to consumers due to surveillance and privacy concerns.
  • The CLARITY Act is particularly relevant to the ETF space, since it addresses the ongoing ambiguity over whether certain tokens fall under the jurisdiction of the SEC or CFTC — essentially the longstanding “security vs. commodity” debate.
  • Additionally, the SEC made a statement in early July addressing some of the disclosure requirements for crypto asset exchange traded products.

The CLARITY Act and the Anti-CBDC Act still need Senate approval. But overall, these regulations have already contributed to improving investor sentiment. I expect to see an influx of approvals later this year — including spot ETFs, multi-token ETFs (discussed in more detail below), staking, and in-kind redemptions — after a period of internal alignment.

…leads to stronger institutional adoption…

Optimism around regulatory support discussed above has led to renewed enthusiasm in the cryptomarkets, particularly stronger institutional interest.

Bitcoin recently crossed $120,000, while spot bitcoin ETFs saw approximately $8 billion in net inflows over the past month. That brings the year-to-date total to over $19 billion. Most of this activity has been concentrated in the iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) which saw around $6 billion in net inflows this past month.

Similarly, ether has rebounded sharply, rising around 60% in the past month. While ether ETFs have seen a total of just under $6 billion in net inflows this year, over half of that — $3 billion — came in just the last month after a previously slow year.

…and product innovation including altcoin ETFs…

Along with more institutional adoption, investor interest is widening to include altcoins and multi-asset crypto exposure.

The REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK), which was launched earlier this month, is both the first spot Solana (SOL-USD) and the first to offer staking. The nine other proposed spot Solana ETFs were filed by the usual players like Fidelity, VanEck, and 21Shares under the ’33 Act — the same structure as both spot bitcoin and ether ETFs. SSK, however, is a ’40 Act fund. This requires it to invest through a diversified portfolio of other securities. Currently, it includes the 21Shares Solana ETP, at around 40% of the fund’s weight. While this means it’s not a “traditional spot product,” SSK gained early traction due to its first-mover advantage, with assets growing to around $100 million within less than a month.

In addition to SSK, leveraged and derivatives-based Solana and XRP products have entered the market, including products from ProShares, Volatility Shares, and Teucrium. While these haven’t taken off significantly, they are still gathering a fair amount of attention, especially given the already crowded market. The Teucrium 2x Long Daily XRP ETF (XXRP) recently saw over $160 million in net inflows over the past month.

…and multi-token ETFs.

The next step for crypto ETFs will likely be multitoken products. Grayscale’s Digital Large Cap Fund (OTC:GDLC), which includes approximately 75% bitcoin, 15% ether, and smaller allocations to Solana, XRP, and Cardano, received initial approval but was placed on hold by the SEC. A similar approval-then-hold process happened to Bitwise’s application to convert its Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund (OTC:BITW) into an ETF. Like GDLC, BITW also holds a large weight in bitcoin and ether (90%) with the remaining 10% in Solana, XRP, Cardano, Avalanche, Chainlink, Bitcoin Cash, Uniswap, and Polkadot.

These pauses aren’t denials. They more likely reflect the need to align with updated SEC regulations, including the guidance issued in early July, which outlined disclosure standards for crypto products. The uniqueness of these multitoken funds stems from their structure since they include tokens that don’t yet have spot ETFs (XRP, Cardano, etc.). That creates two possibilities: 1) SEC approval of multitoken funds could signal green lights for several of these spot ETFs; or 2) alternatively, the small allocations may allow for approval without broader spot fund precedents.

Either way, GDLC and BITW present a test for how the SEC will treat diversified crypto exposure under the evolving framework. A filing for a similar product (Crypto Blue Chip ETF) tied to Trump Media in partnership with Yorkville and Crypto.com also recently made headlines. This product looks similar to GDLC but substitutes Cardano for Cronos (CRO).

Bottom Line: As more regulatory clarity emerges, I expect to see increasing product innovation and broader institutional adoption. Both of those could further solidify crypto’s role in the ETF ecosystem and traditional finance.
